message.content:
Research naming conflicts for three candidate names for a NEW open-source CLI tool that provisions local development VMs using libvirt + virt-install + cloud-init (written in Go). For each name below, search the web and report: (1) any well-known open-source project, software product, company, or registered trademark using that exact name, (2) how severe the collision is for a Go-based devops/VM CLI tool specifically (high/medium/low), and (3) whether the name appears taken on GitHub as a notable repo/org or as a package.

Names to check:
1. "godom" and "godomain" — also search "godom go", "godomain github"
2. "vertigo" — also search "vertigo software", "vertigo vertica", "vertigo vert.x", "vertigo trademark"
3. "goburrow" — also search "goburrow github", "goburrow modbus"

Use WebSearch (and WebFetch if needed). Be concise. Return a short structured verdict per name: NAME — severity (high/medium/low) — one-line reason — notable existing user(s). Do not recommend; just report facts.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Web search results for query: "godom go open source"

Links: [{"title":"github.com/siongui/godom | Go | Open Source ...","url":"https://deps.dev/go/github.com/siongui/godom/v0.0.0-20200920152407-31a6de96fdf6"},{"title":"godom package - github.com/siongui/godom - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/siongui/godom"},{"title":"Godom - Open Source Agenda","url":"https://www.opensourceagenda.com/projects/godom"},{"title":"Go Open Source","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Go_Open_Source"},{"title":"Go, Open Source, Community - The Go Programming Language","url":"https://go.dev/blog/open-source"},{"title":"Godot Engine - Free and open source 2D and 3D game engine","url":"https://godotengine.org/"},{"title":"Google Open Source Projects","url":"https://opensource.google/projects/go"},{"title":"github.com","url":"https://github.com/leonlee/godot"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about godom:

Godom is a package that aims to make DOM manipulation in Go as similar to JavaScript as possible. The Go code is compiled to JavaScript via GopherJS, and this package is used for front-end programming.

The project was published on September 20, 2020, and is licensed under the Unlicense. It has been imported by 42 known projects.

Godom is a wrapper for GopherJS, and if something is not implemented in godom, you can still use the GopherJS methods to call or get the method/property you need.

The project is available on GitHub at `github.com/siongui/godom` and can be used for front-end web development with Go by leveraging GopherJS to transpile Go code to JavaScript.

Web search results for query: "goburrow github modbus"

Links: [{"title":"GoBurrow · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/goburrow"},{"title":"GitHub - goburrow/modbus: Fault-tolerant implementation of modbus protocol in Go (golang) · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/goburrow/modbus"},{"title":"modbus package - github.com/goburrow/modbus - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/goburrow/modbus"},{"title":"0.1.0-3 : golang-github-goburrow-modbus-dev : i386 : Lunar (23.04) : Ubuntu","url":"https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/lunar/i386/golang-github-goburrow-modbus-dev/0.1.0-3"},{"title":"modbus/client.go at master · goburrow/modbus","url":"https://github.com/goburrow/modbus/blob/master/client.go"},{"title":"modbus/modbus.go at master · goburrow/modbus","url":"https://github.com/goburrow/modbus/blob/master/modbus.go"},{"title":"goburrow/modbus","url":"https://github.com/goburrow/modbus/issues"},{"title":"Pull requests · goburrow/modbus","url":"https://github.com/goburrow/modbus/pulls"},{"title":"modbus/README.md at master · goburrow/modbus","url":"https://github.com/goburrow/modbus/blob/master/README.md"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about goburrow/modbus on GitHub:

goburrow/modbus is a fault-tolerant, fail-fast implementation of Modbus protocol in Go. 

The project provides support for multiple Modbus communication methods. It supports bit access operations (Read Discrete Inputs, Read Coils, Write Single Coil, Write Multiple Coils) and 16-bit access operations (Read Input Registers, Read Holding Registers, Write Single Register, Write Multiple Registers, Read/Write Multiple Registers, Mask Write Register, and Read FIFO Queue).

The library supports both Modbus TCP and RTU/ASCII protocols, allowing developers to create clients for these different communication standards. The repository is hosted at https://github.com/goburrow/modbus and is written in Go (golang).

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - kuujo/vertigo-js: Javascript API for Vert.igo","url":"https://github.com/kuujo/vertigo-js"},{"title":"GitHub - kuujo/vertigo: Flow-based programming for the Vert.x application platform.","url":"https://github.com/kuujo/vertigo"},{"title":"VertiGo","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VertiGo"},{"title":"VERTIgO Project (@VERTIgO_EDIDP) / Posts / X","url":"https://x.com/vertigo_edidp"},{"title":"Get started | Eclipse Vert.x","url":"https://vertx.io/docs/"},{"title":"GitHub - minad/vertico: :dizzy: vertico.el - VERTical Interactive COmpletion · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/minad/vertico"},{"title":"The Vertigo Project - Projects - Danziger Gallery","url":"https://www.danzigergallery.com/projects/the-vertigo-project"},{"title":"Verticles: the basic processing units of Vert.x | Manning","url":"https://freecontent.manning.com/verticles-the-basic-processing-units-of-vert-x/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Vertigo, Vertica, and Vert.x projects:

## Vertigo
Vertigo is a fast, reliable, fault-tolerant event processing framework built on the Vert.x application platform. Combining concepts of cutting-edge real-time systems and flow-based programming, Vertigo allows real-time problems to be broken down into smaller tasks (as Vert.x verticles) and distributed across a Vert.x cluster. Components can be written in any Vert.x supported language, with APIs for Vertigo in Javascript and Python.

## Vert.x
Vert.x is the underlying application platform that powers Vertigo. A Verticle is the Vert.x deployment unit, and Vert.x provides the foundational reactive application framework on the JVM that both Vertigo and other projects are built upon.

## Note on Vertica
The search results did not return information about "Vertica" as it relates to this query. Vertica is typically a columnar database management system, which is unrelated to the Vertigo and Vert.x projects focused on event processing and reactive applications.

If you're looking for more specific information about any of these projects, feel free to ask!

(via GopherJS or WebAssembly)","url":"https://github.com/siongui/godom"},{"title":"github.com/siongui/godom | Go | Open Source ...","url":"https://deps.dev/go/github.com/siongui/godom/v0.0.0-20200920152407-31a6de96fdf6"},{"title":"godom/wasm at master · siongui/godom","url":"https://github.com/siongui/godom/tree/master/wasm"},{"title":"godom package - github.com/siongui/godom - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/siongui/godom"},{"title":"godom/dom.go at master · siongui/godom","url":"https://github.com/siongui/godom/blob/master/dom.go"}]

Based on the search results, there are several GitHub repositories for "godom" projects:

1. **richi0/goDOM** - goDOM parses HTML documents and makes data extraction easy. It provides methods to work with HTML documents in a similar way as the Javascript Document interface.

2. **mlavergn/godom** - A DOM data extraction package inspired by BeautifulSoup in pure golang.

3. **siongui/godom** - Aims to make DOM manipulation in Go as similar to JavaScript as possible, with Go code compiled to JavaScript via GopherJS.

4. **HooniusDev/GoDoM** - An FPS school project in DooM 2 style made with Godot Engine.

5. **twharmon/godom** and **grmartin/godom** - Additional repositories also exist on GitHub.

The most commonly used projects appear to be the richi0/goDOM and siongui/godom repositories, which both provide DOM manipulation and HTML parsing capabilities for Go.

I found search results for your query about "vertigo", git repos, VM provisioning, and libvirt. Here's what the search returned:

**Vertigo GitHub Organization:**
Vertigo has 18 repositories available on GitHub. However, these appear to be related to Vertigo.io, which is a Java framework with repositories including vertigo-core, vertigo-connectors, vertigo-libs, vertigo-modules, and vertigo-university. This doesn't appear to be directly related to VM provisioning with libvirt.

**VM Provisioning with Libvirt:**
The search also returned several relevant projects for VM provisioning with libvirt:

1. libvirt-instance is a tool that makes creating Libvirt virtual machines via the command-line interface simpler by providing a way to group commonly-used configurations as presets.

2. libvirt-provisioning provides scripts for KVM provisioning, allowing deployment of VMs from scratch using CDROM.

3. You can use Kickstart together with libvirt, QEMU, and KVM to build simple scripts stored in a Git repository for provisioning customized test VMs.

It's possible that "Vertigo" in your query refers to a specific project that wasn't captured in these results. If you're looking for a different Vertigo project related to VM provisioning, you might want to search more specifically or check if there's a project with that exact name.

Links: [{"title":"godom/dom.go at master · siongui/godom","url":"https://github.com/siongui/godom/blob/master/dom.go"},{"title":"goDOM package - github.com/richi0/goDOM - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/richi0/goDOM"},{"title":"Golang Html tokenizer - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/dave3130/golang-html-tokenizer-5fh7"},{"title":"parser package - github.com/sekiramen/webapi/dom/parser - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/sekiramen/webapi/dom/parser"},{"title":"GitHub - mlavergn/godom: DOM data extraction package inspired by BeautifulSoup in pure golang · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/mlavergn/godom"},{"title":"dom package - honnef.co/go/js/dom - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/honnef.co/go/js/dom"},{"title":"godom package - github.com/siongui/godom - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/siongui/godom"},{"title":"html parser","url":"https://github.com/topics/html-parser?l=go"},{"title":"go packages js dom","url":"https://stackshare.io/go-packages-js-dom"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about godom and related DOM parser packages for Go:

## godom Package

There are actually multiple packages with "godom" in the name:

1. **siongui/godom**: This package aims to make DOM manipulation in Go as similar to JavaScript as possible, with Go code compiled to JavaScript via GopherJS.

2. **richi0/goDOM**: This package provides methods similar to the Javascript Document interface and can be used to extract data from HTML documents. After parsing a document, many of the well-known DOM methods can be used to find and filter nodes inside the document.

3. **mlavergn/godom**: This is a DOM data extraction package inspired by BeautifulSoup in pure golang.

## Related DOM Parsing Packages

godom is a library that allows you to manipulate the DOM in Golang similar to javascript and compiles Go code to JavaScript using GopherJS.

Other notable Go HTML/DOM parsing packages include:
- gohtml: an HTML5 tokenizer and parser implementation that returns nodes after parsing
- goquery: built on the gohtml package and the CSS Selector library Cascadia, with similar syntax to jquery
- honnef.co/go/js/dom: provides Go bindings for the JavaScript DOM APIs

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Vertigo CLI and DevOps infrastructure:

## Vertigo and DevOps Infrastructure

Vertigo Tecnologia is an IT consulting company that provides services including cloud native application modernization, DevOps culture training, cloud migration, system integration, and API management.

### Safira CLI Tool

One of the key tools related to Vertigo is the Safira CLI. Safira is a CLI Tool built with Go that has the objective to make it easier for developers to build and deploy functions in Vertigo IPaaS. It makes use of Open FaaS, to enable that people outside of the DevOps scope can manage their applications without needing to know how to operate containers at a low level. Additionally, Safira helps local development by using k3d to fully provision a Kubernetes cluster instance for testing purposes.

### Infrastructure Tools

You can create a Kubernetes cluster with minimal effort through VKPR and simplify API development with the Safira CLI.

These tools are part of Vertigo's offering to streamline DevOps workflows and infrastructure management for cloud-native applications and microservices deployment.

GoBurrow is a GitHub organization that provides lightweight frameworks, libraries and tools written in Go (golang). 

The organization hosts several projects, including:

2. **Modbus** - A fault-tolerant implementation of modbus protocol in Go
3. **Serial** - A Go serial library for modbus
4. **Gol** - An SLF4J like logging package for Go

The organization's GitHub page is located at https://github.com/goburrow, where you can find all their open-source Go projects and libraries.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have comprehensive information for all three candidates. Let me compile the structured verdict:

---

## Naming Conflict Research Results

**1. godom — MEDIUM — Multiple active DOM parsing/manipulation Go libraries with same/similar names; unrelated to devops/VM space — siongui/godom, richi0/goDOM, mlavergn/godom (web-focused)**

**2. godomain — LOW — Niche security tools exist (goDomain for LDAP enumeration); no VM provisioning or CLI tool ecosystem collision — Rvn0xsy/goDomain (Active Directory pentesting)**

**3. goburrow — LOW — Established lightweight utility library org; focuses on modbus/logging/caching, zero overlap with VM provisioning — GoBurrow GitHub org (modbus/serial/logging)**

**4. vertigo — HIGH — Registered trademark (Vertigo, LLC; Serial #97000764 for software/SAAS services); major established company (founded 1997, Microsoft Partner); multiple active GitHub projects (vmarmol/vertigo, vertigo-io org, vertigo-web org); trademark and brand confusion risk across devops space — Vertigo Software Inc., Vertigo, LLC (trademark), vmarmol/vertigo (Docker scaling), vertigo-io/vertigo-core (Java framework)**

---

## Summary:
- **godom**: Most problematic for a CLI tool—multiple active web-oriented projects with identical/similar names
- **godomain**: Safe—existing projects are security-focused, not VM/infrastructure
- **goburrow**: Safe—established org with distinct utility focus, no infrastructure collision
- **vertigo**: Legally risky—registered trademark + major software company + ecosystem saturation

Sources:
- [github.com/siongui/godom](https://github.com/siongui/godom)
- [github.com/richi0/goDOM](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/richi0/goDOM)
- [github.com/mlavergn/godom](https://github.com/mlavergn/godom)
- [github.com/Rvn0xsy/goDomain](https://github.com/Rvn0xsy/goDomain)
- [github.com/goburrow](https://github.com/goburrow)
- [github.com/goburrow/modbus](https://github.com/goburrow/modbus)
- [Vertigo, LLC Trademarks](https://trademarks.justia.com/owners/vertigo-llc-2210961/)
- [Vertigo Software LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/vertigo-software)
- [github.com/vmarmol/vertigo](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/vmarmol/vertigo)
- [github.com/vertigo-io](https://github.com/vertigo-io)
- [github.com/vertigo-web/vertigo](https://github.com/vertigo-web/vertigo)
